title: excludedCommands はあるが Bash(*) が無い -> 行に excluded を含んでも非excluded の後段は permission でゲートされ ask になる (ask / F9 の増幅要因が Bash(*) だと分離)

probes.prompt: Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo hi && cat /etc/hostname > $HOME/lab-h.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.
probes.result_text: The command was blocked with the error: `probe: recording ask, denying to avoid side effects`

hypothesis: excludedCommands only relaxes the sandbox layer, not the permission layer. With allow narrowed to Bash(echo *) (no Bash(*)), the composite line whose second subcommand `cat` is not allowed hits an approval prompt (ask); the whole-line escape does NOT happen automatically. So S5-b's danger is the PRODUCT of excludedCommands AND a broad Bash(*) allow, exactly mirroring how S5-e isolates the allowUnsandboxedCommands path. The operational rule 'don't use Bash(*)' is causally grounded for BOTH escape paths.
